questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Purchase consideration — net payment
questions.stem: Kaveri Paints Ltd takes over Penna Coatings Ltd, which has 80,000 equity shares of ₹10 each. Terms: every 5 Penna shares fetch 2 equity shares of Kaveri (face value ₹10, issued at ₹18) plus ₹3 cash per Penna share held. Kaveri also agrees to pay Penna's trade creditors of ₹2,00,000 directly. Purchase consideration under the net payment method is:

options.explanation: Shares: 80,000 × 2/5 = 32,000 shares × ₹18 = ₹5,76,000; cash: 80,000 × ₹3 = ₹2,40,000; total ₹8,16,000. The creditor payment never enters purchase consideration.
This values the 32,000 shares at ₹10 face value — shares issued as consideration are taken at their issue price of ₹18.
This adds the ₹2,00,000 paid to creditors — para 3(g) counts only payments to SHAREHOLDERS.
This is the share element alone — the ₹3-per-share cash to equity holders is also consideration.

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · What counts as consideration
questions.stem: Under para 3(g) of AS 14, which of the following is NOT part of purchase consideration?

options.text: Equity shares issued by the transferee to the transferor's preference shareholders
options.explanation: Preference shareholders are shareholders — anything issued to them counts.

options.text: Amounts the transferee agrees to pay the transferor's debenture holders
options.explanation: Debenture holders are lenders, not shareholders. A debenture settlement is a liability taken over — it affects net assets, never purchase consideration.

options.text: Cash paid by the transferee to the transferor's equity shareholders
options.explanation: Cash to equity shareholders is squarely within the definition.

options.text: Preference shares issued by the transferee to the transferor's equity shareholders
options.explanation: Securities issued to shareholders count, whatever their class.

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Purchase consideration — net assets
questions.stem: Sharda Ceramics Ltd acquires the business of Betwa Tiles Ltd. Assets taken over are agreed at ₹52,00,000 and liabilities assumed at ₹17,00,000. Betwa's balance sheet also shows share capital of ₹20,00,000 and reserves of ₹8,00,000. Purchase consideration under the net assets method is:

options.explanation: This ignores the liabilities assumed — the transferee pays for the NET of assets over outside liabilities.
This deducts capital and reserves (₹28,00,000) as well — share capital and reserves are not outside liabilities.
Net assets method: agreed value of assets taken over ₹52,00,000 − liabilities assumed ₹17,00,000 = ₹35,00,000. Capital and reserves never enter the computation.
This deducts capital + reserves instead of the liabilities assumed — the deduction is for OUTSIDE liabilities only.

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Purchase consideration — share exchange
questions.stem: Mandovi Breweries Ltd issues 3 of its equity shares (₹10 each, agreed issue value ₹24) for every 5 shares held in Zuari Bottling Ltd, which has 2,50,000 equity shares outstanding. Purchase consideration is:

options.explanation: This is Zuari's paid-up capital — the consideration is what the transferee gives, not what the transferor had.
Shares issued = 2,50,000 × 3/5 = 1,50,000; at ₹24 each, consideration = ₹36,00,000.
This prices ALL 2,50,000 Zuari shares at ₹24 — only the 1,50,000 Mandovi shares actually issued are consideration.
This takes the 1,50,000 shares at ₹10 face value — consideration uses the agreed issue value of ₹24.

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Merger conditions — cash for fractions
questions.stem: For an amalgamation to qualify as a merger under para 3(e) of AS 14, the consideration to the transferor's equity shareholders must be discharged wholly by equity shares of the transferee, EXCEPT that cash may be paid:

options.text: To shareholders who dissent from the scheme
options.explanation: Dissenting shareholders paid in cash would break the wholly-by-equity-shares condition beyond fractions — para 3(e) has no dissenter carve-out.

options.text: Towards the transferor's preference shareholders
options.explanation: The wholly-in-equity condition governs EQUITY shareholders; preference shareholders are outside condition (iii) altogether.

options.text: Up to 10% of the total consideration
options.explanation: There is no percentage-based cash allowance in AS 14 — only fractions.

options.text: For fractional share entitlements
options.explanation: Condition (iii) permits cash only in respect of fractional shares — a shareholder entitled to 12.5 shares gets 12 shares and the half-share in cash.

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Merger conditions — the 90% base
questions.stem: Vindhya Alloys Ltd proposes to absorb Satpura Castings Ltd, which has 2,00,000 equity shares of ₹10. Vindhya already holds 15,000 Satpura shares. For the amalgamation to qualify as a merger, holders of at least how many Satpura shares (by face value count) must become equity shareholders of Vindhya?

options.text: 1,50,000 shares
options.explanation: 75% is the special-resolution threshold under company law, not the AS 14 merger test — AS 14 asks for 90%.

options.text: 1,66,500 shares
options.explanation: Shares already held by the transferee are excluded: base = 2,00,000 − 15,000 = 1,85,000; 90% × 1,85,000 = 1,66,500.

options.text: 1,80,000 shares
options.explanation: This is 90% of ALL shares — the 15,000 shares Vindhya already holds must be excluded from the base first.

options.text: 1,85,000 shares
options.explanation: That is the whole eligible base — the condition requires 90% of it, not all of it.

questions.stem: In an amalgamation in the nature of purchase, Son Valley Cements Ltd pays purchase consideration of ₹60,00,000 for net assets of the transferor valued at ₹52,00,000. The difference is recorded as:

options.text: Capital reserve of ₹8,00,000
options.explanation: Capital reserve arises when net assets EXCEED consideration — here the direction is the other way.

options.text: Goodwill of ₹8,00,000
options.explanation: Consideration exceeds net assets by ₹8,00,000 — the excess paid over what was received is goodwill.

options.text: Charge to the Statement of P&L of ₹8,00,000
options.explanation: The excess is capitalised as goodwill and amortised — it is not expensed at once.

options.text: Goodwill of ₹52,00,000
options.explanation: Net assets are what was received, not the unpaid-for excess — goodwill is only the ₹8,00,000 difference.

questions.stem: Gomti Glass Ltd takes over assets of Sarayu Bottles Ltd agreed at ₹61,00,000 and liabilities of ₹13,00,000, for a purchase consideration of ₹42,00,000 (amalgamation in the nature of purchase). The transferee records:

options.text: Capital reserve of ₹6,00,000
options.explanation: Net assets = 61,00,000 − 13,00,000 = ₹48,00,000; consideration ₹42,00,000 is LESS, so the ₹6,00,000 shortfall is capital reserve.

options.text: Capital reserve of ₹48,00,000
options.explanation: ₹48,00,000 is the net assets figure itself — only the difference from consideration becomes capital reserve.

options.text: Goodwill of ₹6,00,000
options.explanation: Goodwill needs consideration to EXCEED net assets — here the transferee paid less than it received.

options.text: Goodwill of ₹19,00,000
options.explanation: This compares gross assets (₹61,00,000) with consideration and mislabels the direction — liabilities must be deducted first.

questions.stem: Goodwill arising on an amalgamation in the nature of purchase should, per AS 14, be amortised over:

options.text: A period not exceeding five years, unless a somewhat longer period can be justified
options.explanation: AS 14 prescribes systematic amortisation over not more than five years, with a longer period only where justification exists.

options.text: The year of amalgamation itself, as an immediate write-off
options.explanation: Immediate write-off is not the AS 14 treatment — the standard requires systematic amortisation.

options.text: A period not exceeding ten years in all cases
options.explanation: Ten years is the AS 26 rebuttable presumption for other intangibles — AS 14's own rule for amalgamation goodwill is five years.

options.text: No period — it is tested annually for impairment instead
options.explanation: Impairment-only treatment is the Ind AS 103 world; AS 14 requires amortisation.

questions.stem: In a merger accounted under the pooling of interests method, Yamuna Fabrics Ltd issues share capital of ₹25,00,000 to the shareholders of Chambal Weaves Ltd, whose paid-up capital was ₹18,00,000 equity plus ₹2,00,000 preference. The difference is:

options.text: ₹5,00,000 recorded as goodwill
options.explanation: Pooling NEVER creates goodwill — the difference goes to reserves.

options.text: ₹5,00,000 added to reserves
options.explanation: Reserves are INCREASED only when capital issued is less than the capital cancelled — here it is more.

options.text: ₹5,00,000 deducted from reserves
options.explanation: Capital issued ₹25,00,000 − transferor capital ₹20,00,000 (equity + preference) = ₹5,00,000, adjusted against reserves — issued more capital than was cancelled, so reserves shrink.

options.text: ₹7,00,000 deducted from reserves
options.explanation: This ignores the ₹2,00,000 preference capital — the comparison uses the transferor's entire paid-up capital.

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Reserves under purchase method
questions.stem: In an amalgamation in the nature of purchase, which reserves of the transferor company appear in the transferee's financial statements?

options.text: No reserves under any circumstances
options.explanation: Almost — statutory reserves are the one exception, kept alive through the Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve.

options.text: All reserves, at their carrying amounts
options.explanation: Carrying every reserve across is the POOLING treatment — purchase method records none except statutory reserves.

options.text: Only the general reserve and P&L balance
options.explanation: These are precisely the reserves that are NOT recorded under the purchase method.

options.text: Only statutory reserves, via the Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve mechanism
options.explanation: Purchase method drops all transferor reserves except statutory reserves, which retain their identity as long as the governing statute requires.

questions.sourceRef: AS 14 — treatment of reserves under purchase method

questions.stem: The Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve created while incorporating a transferor's statutory reserve is presented as:

options.text: A separate line item with a negative balance within reserves — not set off against the statutory reserve
options.explanation: It must appear as its own (negative) head under reserves; netting it against the statutory reserve is expressly not permitted. Both are reversed when the statutory retention period ends.

options.text: An intangible asset amortised over five years
options.explanation: Five-year amortisation belongs to goodwill on amalgamation — the AAR is a reserves-side item.

options.text: A deduction from the statutory reserve it relates to
options.explanation: Set-off against the statutory reserve is exactly what the separate-line-item requirement forbids.

options.text: A contingent liability in the notes
options.explanation: It is a recognised (negative) component of reserves, not an off-balance-sheet disclosure.

questions.sourceRef: AS 14 — statutory reserves; MCA amendment 2016 (Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve)

questions.stem: On the date of absorption, the transferee's trade receivables are ₹8,00,000 and the transferor's are ₹5,00,000. Included in the transferee's receivables is ₹1,20,000 due from the transferor. Combined trade receivables in the post-amalgamation balance sheet are:

options.explanation: Adding without elimination leaves a receivable the combined entity owes itself — mutual owings must be knocked out.
This eliminates ₹1,20,000 twice — the mutual balance is removed once from receivables (and once from payables).
8,00,000 + 5,00,000 = ₹13,00,000, less the ₹1,20,000 mutual owing eliminated (Trade Payables Dr → To Trade Receivables) = ₹11,80,000.
This drops the transferor's receivables entirely — only the ₹1,20,000 mutual balance is eliminated.

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Unrealised profit in stock
questions.stem: After absorbing Tawa Traders, the transferee holds inventory of ₹2,40,000 purchased from Tawa, which invoiced goods at cost plus 20%. The unrealised profit to be eliminated from stock is:

options.explanation: 20% of the invoice value treats the mark-up as a margin ON SELLING PRICE — it is 20% on COST, so the fraction is 20/120.
That is the COST of the stock — the elimination removes only the profit layer sitting above cost.
Margin on invoice price = 20/120; unrealised profit = 2,40,000 × 20/120 = ₹40,000, bringing the stock down to its ₹2,00,000 cost to the group.
This applies 25% to invoice value — 25% on cost would equal 20% on selling price, the reverse of the given mark-up.

questions.stem: External reconstruction refers to:

options.text: An existing company absorbing another existing company
options.explanation: That is absorption — no new company is formed at all.

options.text: Two existing companies combining into a newly formed third company
options.explanation: That is amalgamation in the strict sense — external reconstruction involves only ONE existing company being taken over.

options.text: A new company formed specifically to take over the business of one existing company, which is then wound up
options.explanation: One seller, one purpose-built buyer — the existing company transfers its business to the newly floated company and dies. The accounting follows AS 14 like any amalgamation.

options.text: A company reorganising its capital internally by writing down shares against accumulated losses
options.explanation: Writing down capital within the same entity is INTERNAL reconstruction — a different chapter with no transferee company.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 13 scope — amalgamation, absorption, external reconstruction

questions.stem: While closing the transferor's books, the balance of the Realisation Account (profit or loss on realisation) is transferred to:

options.text: The Transferee Company Account
options.explanation: The transferee's account records only the consideration receivable and its discharge — never the realisation result.

options.text: The Statement of Profit and Loss
options.explanation: The transferor is being wound up — there is no continuing P&L to absorb the result.

options.text: The Equity Shareholders Account
options.explanation: Realisation profit or loss belongs to the residual owners — it joins capital and free reserves in the Equity Shareholders Account, which must then close to nil.

options.text: Capital Reserve
options.explanation: Capital reserve on amalgamation arises in the TRANSFEREE's books under the purchase method, not in the dying company's ledger.

questions.stem: Purna Pipes Ltd transfers to its acquirer assets with a book value of ₹50,00,000 and liabilities of ₹18,00,000, for purchase consideration of ₹36,00,000. Liquidation expenses of ₹50,000 are borne by Purna itself. Profit on realisation is:

options.text: Loss of ₹3,50,000
options.explanation: The consideration exceeds the net book value given up — the result is a profit, not a loss.
Consideration 36,00,000 − net book value taken over (50,00,000 − 18,00,000 = 32,00,000) − expenses 50,000 = ₹3,50,000.
This ADDS the expenses instead of deducting them — expenses borne by the transferor reduce the realisation profit.
This forgets the liquidation expenses, which the transferor bears and debits to the Realisation Account.

questions.stem: The pooling of interests method of accounting may be applied:

options.text: Only to an amalgamation that satisfies all five conditions of para 3(e) — a merger
options.explanation: Pooling is reserved for mergers; any amalgamation failing even one condition is a purchase and must use the purchase method.

options.text: Whenever the two companies are in the same line of business
options.explanation: Genuine pooling requires the para 3(e) conditions — same industry is not among them.

options.text: To any amalgamation, at the transferee's option
options.explanation: The method is dictated by the classification, never by choice.

options.text: Only when a new company is formed to take over both businesses
options.explanation: A merger can equally be an absorption by an existing company — what matters is the five conditions, not whether a new entity exists.

questions.stem: Hooghly Shipping Ltd (net assets ₹96,00,000; 4,00,000 equity shares) absorbs Damodar Barges Ltd (net assets ₹30,00,000; 2,50,000 equity shares). Shares are exchanged on intrinsic values. The number of Hooghly shares issued is:

options.text: 5,00,000 shares
options.explanation: This inverts the ratio to 2-for-1 — the cheaper Damodar share earns HALF a Hooghly share, not two.

options.text: 62,500 shares
options.explanation: 1-for-4 has no basis — the intrinsic values stand in a 1:2 relation, not 1:4.

options.text: 2,50,000 shares
options.explanation: A 1:1 swap ignores that a Hooghly share is worth twice a Damodar share.

options.text: 1,25,000 shares
options.explanation: Intrinsic values: Hooghly 96,00,000 ÷ 4,00,000 = ₹24; Damodar 30,00,000 ÷ 2,50,000 = ₹12. Ratio ₹12/₹24 = 1 Hooghly share per 2 Damodar shares → 2,50,000 ÷ 2 = 1,25,000 shares.

questions.sourceRef: Intrinsic value / share exchange method (SM Ch 13)

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Liquidation expenses borne by transferee
questions.stem: Where the transferee company agrees to bear the transferor's liquidation expenses, the transferee debits the amount to:

options.text: Purchase consideration, increasing it
options.explanation: Para 3(g) confines consideration to what shareholders receive — reimbursed winding-up costs go to goodwill/capital reserve instead.

options.text: The Realisation Account
options.explanation: The Realisation Account lives in the TRANSFEROR's books — and even there, only expenses the transferor itself bears are debited.

options.text: Goodwill (or Capital Reserve) Account
options.explanation: The expense is part of the cost of acquiring the business but is NOT purchase consideration — it adjusts goodwill or capital reserve directly.

options.text: The Statement of Profit and Loss as an expense
options.explanation: It is an acquisition-related capital adjustment in this chapter's scheme, not a revenue expense of the transferee.

questions.sourceRef: Liquidation expenses treatment (SM Ch 13); AS 14, para 3(g)

questions.stem: Goodwill of ₹2,50,000 arose on an amalgamation in the nature of purchase. Amortising on a straight-line basis over the maximum period AS 14 ordinarily allows, the annual amortisation charge is:

options.explanation: AS 14's ordinary ceiling is five years: 2,50,000 ÷ 5 = ₹50,000 per year.
Full write-off in year one is not required — the standard spreads the charge over the amortisation period.
Twenty years has no basis in AS 14 — the ordinary maximum is five.
Ten years is the AS 26 presumption for other intangibles — AS 14's own ordinary maximum is five years.

questions.stem: Under the pooling of interests method, the transferor's assets and liabilities are incorporated in the transferee's books at:

options.text: Values agreed between the two boards, whatever they are
options.explanation: Agreed-value incorporation is purchase-method territory; a merger carries book values forward.

options.text: Their existing carrying amounts, adjusted only to achieve uniformity of accounting policies
options.explanation: Pooling continues the old book values; the sole permitted adjustment aligns accounting policies (e.g. restating inventory from weighted average to FIFO).

options.text: Realisable values net of expected selling costs
options.explanation: Realisable value is a liquidation basis — a merger assumes the business continues as before.

options.text: Fair values on the date of amalgamation
options.explanation: Fair-value allocation belongs to the PURCHASE method — pooling preserves book values.

questions.sourceRef: AS 14 — pooling of interests method; para 3(e)(v)

questions.stem: In an absorption, the transferee issues shares worth ₹14,00,000 plus cash of ₹2,00,000 to the transferor's equity shareholders, issues shares worth ₹5,00,000 to its preference shareholders, pays the transferor's debenture holders ₹4,00,000, and reimburses liquidation expenses of ₹30,000. Purchase consideration per AS 14 is:

options.explanation: Only payments to shareholders count: 14,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 5,00,000 = ₹21,00,000. Debenture holders and expense reimbursements stay out.
This adds the liquidation-expense reimbursement, which is debited to goodwill/capital reserve, not to consideration.
This wrongly adds the ₹4,00,000 to debenture holders — lenders are not shareholders.
This adds both the debenture payment and the expense reimbursement — para 3(g) excludes each of them.

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Statutory reserves — which method
questions.stem: The Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve mechanism for carrying forward statutory reserves is needed:

options.text: Under neither method — statutory reserves always lapse
options.explanation: Statutory reserves must be maintained while the governing statute requires — that is exactly why the AAR entry exists.

options.text: Under both methods, in every amalgamation
options.explanation: It is redundant under pooling — the statutory reserve arrives with all the other reserves.

options.text: Only under the pooling of interests method
options.explanation: Backwards — pooling has no need for it, since reserves are carried forward as a matter of course.

options.text: Only under the purchase method — pooling carries all reserves across anyway
options.explanation: In a merger every reserve survives with its identity, so no special machinery is needed; the AAR exists because the purchase method otherwise drops the transferor's reserves.

questions.sourceRef: AS 14 — statutory reserves under purchase method

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Net assets — agreed vs book values
questions.stem: Ulhas Motors Ltd takes over Savitri Auto Ltd: PPE at an agreed ₹30,00,000 (book value ₹26,00,000), inventory at book value ₹5,00,000 (no value agreed), receivables at an agreed ₹5,60,000, and assumes creditors of ₹6,60,000. Net assets taken over amount to:

options.explanation: This forgets to deduct the creditors assumed — net assets are net of outside liabilities taken over.
This uses the ₹26,00,000 book value for PPE — where a value has been agreed, the agreed value prevails.
This omits the inventory — assets with no agreed value still come in, at their book value.
Agreed values where they exist, book values where none was agreed: 30,00,000 + 5,00,000 + 5,60,000 − 6,60,000 = ₹34,00,000.

questions.sourceRef: AS 14 — net assets method (agreed values; book value where not agreed)

questions.stem: After passing the usual acquisition entries, the transferee eliminates an amount the two companies owed each other by:

options.text: No entry — the balances cancel automatically on consolidation
options.explanation: This is a single surviving legal entity, not a consolidation working — an actual journal entry is needed.

options.text: Trade Receivables A/c Dr → To Trade Payables A/c
options.explanation: This GROSSES UP both balances instead of cancelling them — the debit must go to payables.

options.text: Goodwill A/c Dr → To Trade Receivables A/c
options.explanation: A mutual owing is not a valuation adjustment — it cancels against the matching payable, leaving goodwill untouched.

options.text: Trade Payables A/c Dr → To Trade Receivables A/c
options.explanation: The mutual balance sits as a receivable in one ledger and a payable in the other; one entry removes both from the combined books.

questions.stem: Kosi Cables Ltd (general reserve ₹12,00,000) merges with Gandak Wires Ltd (general reserve ₹5,00,000, P&L balance ₹1,80,000, paid-up capital ₹20,00,000) under the pooling of interests method, issuing share capital of ₹22,00,000. If the capital difference is adjusted against general reserve, the combined general reserve is:

options.explanation: Adjustment = 22,00,000 − 20,00,000 = ₹2,00,000. Combined GR = 12,00,000 + 5,00,000 − 2,00,000 = ₹15,00,000; the P&L balance carries forward separately.
This lumps the P&L balance into general reserve AND skips the adjustment — pooling preserves each reserve's identity.
Dropping the transferor's reserve is purchase-method thinking — a merger carries it forward.
This forgets the ₹2,00,000 capital-difference adjustment that pooling requires.

questions.stem: Which single feature, by itself, forces an amalgamation to be classified as being in the nature of PURCHASE?

options.text: The transferee revalued its OWN assets last year
options.explanation: The book-value condition concerns the TRANSFEROR's assets on incorporation — the transferee's own past revaluations are irrelevant.

options.text: Holders of 92% of the transferor's equity face value become transferee shareholders
options.explanation: 92% clears the 90% threshold — this condition is satisfied, not broken.

options.text: The transferor's inventory is restated from weighted average to FIFO to match the transferee's policy
options.explanation: Policy-uniformity adjustments are expressly permitted by condition (v) — they do not disturb merger status.

options.text: Equity shareholders of the transferor receive ₹5 cash per share in addition to equity shares
options.explanation: Cash beyond fractional entitlements breaks condition (iii) — consideration must be wholly equity shares. One failed condition is enough.

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Case: Chenab absorbs Jhelum (purchase method)
questions.case: Chenab Engineering Ltd absorbs Jhelum Forgings Ltd in an amalgamation in the nature of purchase. Jhelum's balance sheet shows: 1,50,000 equity shares of ₹10 fully paid (₹15,00,000); general reserve ₹4,00,000; export profit reserve (maintained under a statutory requirement with three years still to run) ₹1,00,000; 10% debentures ₹6,00,000; trade creditors ₹3,50,000. Chenab takes over all assets at an agreed total of ₹34,00,000 and assumes both the debentures and the creditors. The terms: every 5 Jhelum equity shares fetch 4 equity shares of Chenab (face ₹10, issued at ₹12.50), plus ₹1 cash per Jhelum share held. The CFO wants the acquisition schedule: consideration, net assets, the resulting goodwill or capital reserve, and the fate of Jhelum's reserves.

questions.stem: Purchase consideration is:

options.explanation: This adds the ₹6,00,000 debentures — a liability assumed, which belongs in the net-assets working, not in consideration.
The share element alone — the ₹1-per-share cash to equity holders is consideration too.
This takes the 1,20,000 shares at ₹10 face value — consideration shares are measured at the ₹12.50 issue price.

questions.stem: Net assets taken over amount to:

options.explanation: Gross assets — the debentures and creditors Chenab assumes must be deducted.
This also deducts Jhelum's reserves (₹5,00,000) — reserves are owners' funds, never a deduction in the net-assets working.
This deducts only the debentures — the trade creditors are assumed as well.

questions.stem: The acquisition gives rise to:

options.text: Goodwill of ₹8,00,000
options.explanation: Goodwill would need consideration to be the LARGER number — here Chenab paid less than the net assets received.

options.text: Capital reserve of ₹17,50,000
options.explanation: This compares gross assets ₹34,00,000 with consideration — liabilities assumed must be netted first.

options.text: Capital reserve of ₹8,00,000
options.explanation: Net assets ₹24,50,000 exceed consideration ₹16,50,000 by ₹8,00,000 — the bargain element is capital reserve.

options.text: Neither — the difference adjusts general reserve
options.explanation: Adjusting reserves for the difference is the POOLING plug; a purchase produces goodwill or capital reserve.

questions.stem: To keep Jhelum's export profit reserve alive, Chenab passes:

options.text: Export Profit Reserve Dr ₹1,00,000 → To P&L ₹1,00,000
options.explanation: This RELEASES the reserve to profit — the opposite of preserving it; release happens only when the statutory period ends, and via reversal of the AAR entry.

options.text: Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve Dr ₹5,00,000 → To Reserves ₹5,00,000
options.explanation: ₹5,00,000 sweeps in the general reserve too — only STATUTORY reserves get this treatment; general reserve is simply dropped.

options.text: No entry — statutory reserves lapse on amalgamation
options.explanation: They must be maintained while the statute requires (three years remain here) — that is the whole point of the mechanism.

options.text: Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve Dr ₹1,00,000 → To Export Profit Reserve ₹1,00,000
options.explanation: Statutory reserves retain their identity for the balance of the statutory period; the AAR (a negative reserves line) is the balancing debit.

questions.sourceRef: AS 14 — statutory reserves; Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve

questions.stem: Jhelum's general reserve of ₹4,00,000 appears in Chenab's post-amalgamation balance sheet:

options.text: As part of capital reserve
options.explanation: Capital reserve is the excess of net assets over consideration — it is computed, not assembled from the transferor's reserve balances.

options.text: Merged into Chenab's own general reserve
options.explanation: Carrying reserves across is pooling treatment — this amalgamation is a purchase.

options.text: Nowhere — under the purchase method it is not recorded at all
options.explanation: Non-statutory reserves of the transferor die with it in a purchase; only the export profit reserve survives, via the AAR entry.

options.text: As a negative Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve
options.explanation: The AAR mirrors STATUTORY reserves only — the general reserve simply disappears.

questions.sourceRef: AS 14 — treatment of reserves under purchase method

questions.case: Bhagirathi Cotton Ltd and Alaknanda Spinning Ltd amalgamate. All assets and liabilities of Alaknanda pass to Bhagirathi; holders of 96% of Alaknanda's equity face value become Bhagirathi shareholders; the consideration is wholly equity shares (cash only for fractions); the spinning business will continue; and book values are carried forward. Alaknanda's balance sheet: paid-up equity capital ₹20,00,000 (2,00,000 shares of ₹10); general reserve ₹7,00,000; P&L balance ₹3,00,000. Bhagirathi (general reserve ₹15,00,000) issues 9 of its ₹10 equity shares for every 8 Alaknanda shares held. The capital difference, per the scheme, is adjusted against general reserve.

questions.stem: The amalgamation is accounted for under:

options.text: Either method, at Bhagirathi's option
options.explanation: The method follows the classification automatically — there is no accounting choice.

options.text: The purchase method — only 96% (not 100%) of shareholders joined
options.explanation: The threshold is 90% of the eligible face value; 96% passes comfortably.

options.text: The pooling of interests method — all five para 3(e) conditions are satisfied
options.explanation: All assets/liabilities pass, 96% ≥ 90%, consideration is wholly equity (fractions aside), the business continues, and book values are retained — a merger, so pooling applies.

options.text: The purchase method — cash was paid for fractions
options.explanation: Fractional cash is the one payment condition (iii) expressly permits — it does not break merger status.

questions.stem: Share capital issued by Bhagirathi is:

options.explanation: This issues only the excess over a 1:1 swap — the full 9 shares per 8 held must be issued.
This inverts the ratio to 8-for-9 — Alaknanda holders receive MORE shares than they surrender, not fewer.
A 1:1 swap ignores the stated 9-for-8 exchange ratio.

questions.stem: The amount adjusted against reserves under pooling is:

options.text: Nil — pooling never adjusts reserves
options.explanation: The capital-difference adjustment is precisely the one plug pooling requires.

options.text: ₹2,50,000, added to reserves
options.explanation: Reserves would grow only if capital issued were LESS than the capital cancelled — here it is more.

options.text: ₹2,50,000, recorded as goodwill
options.explanation: Pooling never produces goodwill — the difference always lands in reserves.

options.text: ₹2,50,000, deducted
options.explanation: Capital issued ₹22,50,000 − Alaknanda's capital ₹20,00,000 = ₹2,50,000, deducted from reserves since more capital was issued than cancelled.

questions.stem: Combined general reserve after the merger is:

options.explanation: This skips the ₹2,50,000 adjustment for the excess capital issued.
This applies the adjustment but forgets to add Alaknanda's ₹7,00,000 general reserve first.
Dropping Alaknanda's general reserve is purchase-method treatment — pooling carries it forward.

questions.stem: Alaknanda's P&L balance of ₹3,00,000 is:

options.text: Merged into general reserve
options.explanation: Reserves keep their separate identities under pooling — no relabelling.

options.text: Transferred to Capital Reserve
options.explanation: No capital reserve arises in a pooling — and reserve identities are preserved in any case.

options.text: Carried into Bhagirathi's books as a P&L (retained earnings) balance, identity intact
options.explanation: Pooling preserves the identity of every reserve — P&L balance stays P&L balance.

options.text: Not recorded
options.explanation: Dropping it is purchase-method treatment; in a merger all reserves come across.

questions.sourceRef: AS 14 — pooling of interests method (identity of reserves)

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Case: Closing Tungabhadra's books
questions.case: Tungabhadra Ceramics Ltd transfers its business to Malaprabha Potteries Ltd for a purchase consideration of ₹38,00,000. Its balance sheet on that date: sundry assets ₹42,00,000 (all taken over at book value) and cash ₹4,00,000 (retained); trade creditors ₹9,00,000 (taken over); bank loan ₹3,00,000 (NOT taken over — repaid at book value by Tungabhadra); equity capital ₹25,00,000; general reserve ₹6,00,000; P&L balance ₹3,00,000. Tungabhadra pays its own liquidation expenses of ₹40,000 from the retained cash. You are closing Tungabhadra's books.

questions.stem: Profit on realisation is:

options.explanation: This wrongly deducts the ₹3,00,000 bank loan — a liability repaid at its book value passes through with no profit or loss effect.

Consideration 38,00,000 − net book value taken over (42,00,000 − 9,00,000 = 33,00,000) − liquidation expenses 40,000 = ₹4,60,000. The bank loan, repaid at book value, creates no realisation difference.
This ignores the ₹40,000 liquidation expenses borne by Tungabhadra, which are debited to the Realisation Account.

options.text: Loss of ₹4,60,000
options.explanation: Consideration exceeds the net book value surrendered — the realisation result is a profit.

questions.stem: The ₹40,000 liquidation expenses borne by Tungabhadra are debited to:

options.text: Goodwill Account
options.explanation: Goodwill adjustments for such expenses arise in the TRANSFEREE's books, and only when the transferee bears them.

options.text: The Equity Shareholders Account directly
options.explanation: The Equity Shareholders Account receives the realisation RESULT — individual expenses route through the Realisation Account first.

options.text: The Transferee Company Account
options.explanation: That account tracks consideration due and received — never the transferor's own expenses.

options.text: The Realisation Account
options.explanation: Expenses of winding up borne by the transferor reduce the realisation profit.

questions.stem: The total ultimately credited to (and distributed from) the Equity Shareholders Account is:

options.explanation: This forgets the ₹3,00,000 P&L balance, which is as much shareholders' money as the general reserve.
That is book equity before the realisation profit — the ₹4,60,000 profit belongs to the shareholders too.

Capital 25,00,000 + general reserve 6,00,000 + P&L 3,00,000 + realisation profit 4,60,000 = ₹38,60,000. Cross-check: consideration 38,00,000 + leftover cash (4,00,000 − 3,00,000 − 40,000 = 60,000) = ₹38,60,000. ✓
That is just the purchase consideration — shareholders also receive the leftover retained cash of ₹60,000.

questions.stem: Had Malaprabha (the transferee) agreed to bear the ₹40,000 liquidation expenses instead, then in Tungabhadra's books:

options.text: Purchase consideration would rise by ₹40,000
options.explanation: Para 3(g) confines consideration to payments to shareholders — an expense reimbursement is not one.

options.text: The Equity Shareholders Account would be credited ₹40,000
options.explanation: The shareholders neither pay nor receive this amount — it is the transferee's cost.

options.text: No entry would be made for the expenses
options.explanation: Expenses borne by the transferee bypass the transferor's ledger entirely; the transferee debits them to goodwill or capital reserve.

options.text: The Realisation Account would still be debited ₹40,000
options.explanation: Only expenses the transferor itself bears hit its Realisation Account.

questions.sourceRef: Liquidation expenses — transferee bears (SM Ch 13); AS 14, para 3(g)

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Case: Periyar–Vaigai intrinsic values
questions.case: Periyar Pumps Ltd absorbs Vaigai Valves Ltd, with the share exchange fixed on intrinsic values. Periyar: net assets ₹1,20,00,000; 5,00,000 equity shares of ₹10. Vaigai: net assets ₹18,00,000; 1,50,000 equity shares of ₹10. Shares are issued at intrinsic value; no cash changes hands (assume no fractions arise).

questions.stem: The intrinsic value of one Vaigai share is:

options.explanation: This halves the intrinsic value by applying the exchange ratio prematurely — value the share first, set the ratio second.
Face value is a label, not a valuation — intrinsic value comes from net assets per share.
₹24 is PERIYAR's intrinsic value (1,20,00,000 ÷ 5,00,000) — the question asks for Vaigai's.

questions.stem: The share exchange ratio is:

options.text: 1 Periyar share for every 2 Vaigai shares
options.explanation: ₹12 ÷ ₹24 = ½ — two Vaigai shares carry the value of one Periyar share.

options.text: 1 Periyar share for every 1 Vaigai share
options.explanation: A 1:1 swap ignores that the shares differ in value by a factor of two.

options.text: 3 Periyar shares for every 5 Vaigai shares
options.explanation: 3:5 would need intrinsic values in a 3:5 relation — these stand at 1:2.

options.text: 2 Periyar shares for every 1 Vaigai share
options.explanation: Inverted — this would hand Vaigai holders four times their entitlement.

questions.stem: Periyar issues:

options.text: 75,000 shares
options.explanation: 1,50,000 Vaigai shares ÷ 2 = 75,000 Periyar shares.

options.text: 1,50,000 shares
options.explanation: This is a 1:1 swap — the ratio is 1 for 2.

options.text: 37,500 shares
options.explanation: 1-for-4 halves the correct entitlement — the ratio is 1 for 2.

options.text: 3,00,000 shares
options.explanation: This applies the inverted 2-for-1 ratio.

questions.stem: Purchase consideration (shares issued at intrinsic value) is:

options.explanation: This prices all 1,50,000 VAIGAI shares at ₹24 — only the 75,000 Periyar shares issued constitute consideration.
75,000 shares × ₹24 = ₹18,00,000 — which, satisfyingly, equals Vaigai's net assets: exchanging at intrinsic values prices the business exactly at its net asset value.
This takes the 75,000 shares at ₹10 face value — they are issued at the ₹24 intrinsic value.
This multiplies the 75,000 shares by Vaigai's ₹12 — the shares being issued are Periyar's, valued at ₹24.

questions.stem: State the conditions that must all be satisfied under AS 14 for an amalgamation to be classified as an amalgamation in the nature of merger. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: All assets and all liabilities of the transferor become assets and liabilities of the transferee
Holders of at least 90% (by face value) of the transferor's equity shares — excluding shares the transferee or its subsidiaries/nominees already hold — end up as the transferee's equity shareholders
Consideration to those equity shareholders is discharged wholly by issue of equity shares of the transferee, except cash for fractional shares
The transferee intends to carry on the transferor's business after the amalgamation
No adjustment is intended to the book values of assets and liabilities taken over, except to ensure uniformity of accounting policies

questions.stem: Distinguish amalgamation in the nature of merger from amalgamation in the nature of purchase. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Transfer of assets/liabilities: merger requires ALL assets and liabilities to pass; a purchase need not take everything
Shareholder continuity: merger requires ≥90% (face value) of equity holders to become transferee shareholders; no such requirement in a purchase
Consideration: merger — wholly equity shares (cash only for fractions); purchase — any mix of shares, securities and cash

Accounting: merger → pooling of interests (book values, reserves preserved, no goodwill); purchase → purchase method (agreed/fair values, reserves dropped except statutory, goodwill or capital reserve arises)

questions.stem: Define purchase consideration under AS 14 and briefly explain the methods of computing it. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Definition: aggregate of shares and other securities issued and payments in cash or other assets by the transferee to the SHAREHOLDERS (equity and preference) of the transferor; payments to debenture holders/creditors and expense reimbursements are excluded
Lump sum method — a fixed amount stated in the scheme
skeleton.citation: SM Ch 13 — methods of computing consideration

skeleton.point: Net payment method — aggregate of everything given to equity and preference shareholders (shares at issue price, cash, other assets)
skeleton.citation: SM Ch 13 — methods of computing consideration

skeleton.point: Net assets method — assets taken over at agreed values (book value where none agreed) minus outside liabilities assumed
skeleton.citation: SM Ch 13 — methods of computing consideration

skeleton.point: Intrinsic value / share exchange method — value per share from net assets, exchange ratio set on relative intrinsic values
skeleton.citation: SM Ch 13 — methods of computing consideration

questions.stem: Describe the accounting treatment under the pooling of interests method. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Assets, liabilities and reserves of the transferor recorded at existing carrying amounts, adjusted only for uniformity of accounting policies
Identity of the transferor's reserves is preserved (general reserve remains general reserve, P&L balance remains P&L balance)
Difference between share capital issued (plus any additional consideration) and the transferor's share capital is adjusted in reserves

questions.stem: Describe the accounting treatment under the purchase method, including the treatment of goodwill arising on amalgamation. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Assets and liabilities incorporated at existing carrying amounts OR consideration allocated to identifiable assets and liabilities at fair values on the amalgamation date
Transferor's reserves (other than statutory) are not recorded
skeleton.citation: AS 14 — treatment of reserves under purchase method

skeleton.point: Excess of consideration over net assets = goodwill; shortfall = capital reserve
Goodwill amortised over a period not exceeding five years unless a somewhat longer period can be justified

questions.stem: How are statutory reserves of the transferor dealt with in an amalgamation in the nature of purchase? Give the journal entry and the presentation. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Statutory reserves retain their identity in the transferee's financial statements as long as the governing statute requires — the one exception to reserves being dropped
Entry: Amalgamation Adjustment Reserve A/c Dr → To Statutory Reserve A/c
AAR presented as a separate line item (negative balance) within reserves — not set off against the statutory reserve
When the statutory retention period ends, the entry is reversed and both items disappear

questions.topic: Ch 13 · Amalgamation · Closing the transferor's books
questions.stem: Outline the steps (with the key ledger accounts) for closing the books of the transferor company on amalgamation. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Open Realisation A/c: transfer assets taken over at book values (debit) and liabilities taken over at book values (credit)
Record consideration due: Transferee Co. A/c Dr → To Realisation A/c; on receipt, split into shares/cash received
Settle liabilities not taken over (difference from book value routes through Realisation A/c); debit liquidation expenses borne by the transferor to Realisation A/c
Transfer Realisation profit/loss to Equity Shareholders A/c; settle preference shareholders (premium/discount via Realisation A/c)
Credit capital and free reserves to Equity Shareholders A/c and distribute the shares, cash and residual assets received — the account must close to nil

questions.stem: Explain the treatment of (a) mutual owings between the amalgamating companies and (b) unrealised profit on inventory purchased from the other company, in the transferee's books. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Mutual owings: after the acquisition entries, eliminate the common balance — Trade Payables A/c Dr → To Trade Receivables A/c
No adjustment is needed in the Realisation Account or business-purchase entries for the mutual debt
Unrealised profit: reduce inventory to its cost to the combined entity (profit = margin fraction × invoice value of stock held)
skeleton.citation: SM Ch 13 — adjustment of stock value

skeleton.point: Matching debit: goodwill/capital reserve under the purchase method (or against revenue reserves in a merger)
skeleton.citation: SM Ch 13 — adjustment of stock value

questions.stem: Write a short note on external reconstruction, distinguishing it from amalgamation and absorption. (3 marks)

skeleton.point: External reconstruction: a new company is formed expressly to take over the business of ONE existing company, which is then wound up — same AS 14 accounting
Amalgamation (strict sense): two or more existing companies combine into a newly formed company; absorption: an existing company takes over another existing company with no new entity formed
Contrast with internal reconstruction, where capital is reorganised within the SAME entity and no transferee exists
